critic_raw:
This multi-modal immunotherapy response prediction experiment has several critical methodological flaws.

1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT IGNORES TEMPORAL ORDERING OF CLINICAL TREATMENT AND RESPONSE DATA. The design explicitly rejects temporal splitting because 'treatment response is a fixed post-hoc outcome.' This reasoning is flawed. The timing of data collection matters even for fixed outcomes: clinical documentation practices, treatment protocols, and sequencing panel technologies evolve over 5 years. A model trained on patients from years 1-3 and tested on patients from years 4-5 under random splitting can be tested on patients who chronologically preceded some training patients. This is not a label leakage problem but it allows the model to train on data collected under later practices (better sequencing technology, revised RECIST application) while predicting on earlier patients, artificially inflating apparent generalization. Temporal ordering should at minimum be reported and sensitivity-analyzed.

2. BIOBERT ENCODER IS NOT RETRAINED -- BUT ITS PRETRAINING CORPUS MAY INCLUDE ONCOLOGY PAPERS DESCRIBING MELANOMA RESPONSE PATTERNS. The design uses a pretrained BioBERT encoder frozen during training to encode pathology reports. BioBERT was pretrained on PubMed and PubMed Central, which contain melanoma treatment response literature. If the pathology reports contain standard clinical language that overlaps with patterns described in papers BioBERT was trained on, the encoder may implicitly encode published population-level response statistics that are not available from the patient record alone. This is a form of indirect knowledge leakage that is architecturally invisible but substantively real.

3. N=100 HELD-OUT TEST SET IS INSUFFICIENT FOR SUBGROUP ANALYSIS BY STAGE, ECOG, AND BRAF STATUS. The test set contains approximately 100 patients. The design specifies subgroup analyses by BRAF status, ECOG, TNM stage, and TMB quartile. With four stratification axes on 100 patients, many cells will contain fewer than 10 patients. AUROC computed on cells of this size is unreliable -- the 95% confidence intervals via 1000-replicate bootstrap will be extremely wide, and any subgroup finding (positive or negative) will lack statistical power to support a claim. The design does not acknowledge this limitation.

4. THE BASELINE IS GENOMIC-ONLY NEURAL NETWORK, NOT GENOMIC-ONLY LOGISTIC REGRESSION OR ESTABLISHED CLINICAL PREDICTOR. The baseline uses an identical neural architecture but excludes the clinical and text branches. This design choice controls for capacity on the genomic branch, which is defensible. But it means the baseline is a bespoke genomic neural model rather than an established clinical benchmark (e.g., TMB threshold alone, or published nomogram). If the multi-modal model beats the genomic neural baseline but both underperform a simple TMB threshold, the clinical significance of the multi-modal advantage is zero. The absence of a clinical benchmark baseline leaves the experiment unable to answer whether multi-modal deep learning is useful relative to current practice.

5. RECIST 1.1 RESPONSE AT 12 WEEKS IS A SURROGATE, NOT A CLINICAL ENDPOINT. The design uses response per RECIST 1.1 at 12 weeks as the primary label. RECIST 1.1 response at 12 weeks is an intermediate endpoint; the clinically meaningful outcomes for immunotherapy (overall survival, durable response) have longer time horizons and do not map directly onto 12-week response. In melanoma, some patients who achieve SD at 12 weeks go on to have durable benefit; some PR patients rapidly progress. Predicting RECIST 1.1 response at 12 weeks is not equivalent to predicting clinical benefit, and the hypothesis framing (predicting immunotherapy benefit) exceeds what the label operationalization actually tests.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split ignores temporal ordering of clinical documentation and technology evolution over 5 years
Pretrained BioBERT may encode population-level melanoma response patterns from its PubMed pretraining corpus
N=100 test set is insufficient for reliable subgroup analysis across four stratification axes
Genomic neural baseline omits established clinical benchmarks such as TMB threshold or published nomograms
RECIST 1.1 response at 12 weeks is a surrogate that does not map to durable clinical benefit
n=100 test patients yields wide AUROC confidence intervals; no power analysis justifies detectable effect size
Stratified random split ignores treatment protocol changes over 5 years; protocol-context leakage across time periods
BioBERT on pathology reports may encode post-treatment clinical judgment; pre-treatment cutoff for note inclusion not enforced
Multi-branch neural fusion not compared against logistic regression on concatenated features; architectural complexity unjustified
RECIST 12-week response is a surrogate endpoint not validated against durable clinical benefit or overall survival
Multi-site genomic batch correction commonly uses all samples before splitting, creating leakage into training set despite stated intentions
Test set of n~100 yields AUROC confidence intervals too wide (~±0.10-0.15) to detect realistic multi-modal vs. genomic-only differences
Genomic-only baseline uses same deep neural architecture as full model — unjustified capacity for 500 patients, should be regularized logistic regression
BioBERT fixed embeddings may not transfer to melanoma-specific pathology and RECIST response language
Binarizing RECIST 1.1 response collapses clinically important stable disease vs. progressive disease distinction
